blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VIII
THE RIEMANN-ROCH THEOREMby Pierre BERTHELOT

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of schemes. We say that \(f\) is a complete intersection morphism if for every point \(x\) of \(X\), there exist an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) and a smooth \(Y\)-scheme \(V\), such that the restriction of \(f\) to \(U\) factors as:

\[\begin{array}{ccc}&U\xrightarrow{i}V&\\&\searrow\quad\swarrow&\\&Y&\end{array}\]

where \(i\) is a regular immersion (VII 1.4).
Up to replacing \(V\) by an open set, we may assume that \(i\) is a regular closed immersion.

In this definition we may replace \(V\) by a vector bundle \(Y[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\). Indeed, the notion being local, we may assume that \(V\) is affine, and that its image in \(Y\) is contained in an affine open set \(W\) of \(Y\). There then exist an integer \(n\) and a closed \(W\)-immersion \(V\longrightarrow W[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\), whence a \(Y\)-immersion \(V\longrightarrow Y[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\), which is regular by VII 1.10. Since the composite of two regular immersions is regular, we thus find a regular \(Y\)-immersion \(U\longrightarrow Y[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) factoring \(f\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a complete intersection morphism. Then every \(Y\)-immersion \(i:X\longrightarrow Z\) of \(X\) into a smooth \(Y\)-scheme is regular.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.

Consider the commutative diagram:

\[\begin{array}{ccc}&&V'\\&{}^{j}\nearrow&\downarrow p\\X&\xrightarrow{i}&V\end{array}\]

where \(i\) and \(j\) are immersions, and \(p\) is a smooth morphism. Then for \(i\) to be a regular immersion, it is necessary and sufficient that \(j\) be a regular immersion.
i) Suppose \(i\) regular. Set \(X'=V'\times_V X\). The datum of \(j\) defines a section \(j'\) of \(X'\) over \(X\), such that \(j=i'\circ j'\):

Now \(j'\) is a regular immersion, since it is a section of a smooth morphism (VII 1.10); \(i'\) is a regular immersion since it is deduced from \(i\) by a smooth, hence flat, base change (VII 1.5). Therefore \(j\) is a regular immersion (VII 1.7).
ii) Suppose \(j\) regular, and \(p\) étale. Then \(j'\) is an open immersion (EGA IV 17.9.3), hence an isomorphism from \(X\) onto \(j'(X)\); set \(U=j'(X)\).

Let \(i''\) be the restriction of \(i'\) to \(U\), and let \(U'\) be an open set of \(V'\) such that \(U=U'\cap X'\). We then have: \(i''=j\circ(j')^{-1}\), so \(i''\) is a regular immersion of \(U\) into \(U'\). Now the morphism \(U'\longrightarrow V\), being smooth, is flat and of finite presentation, hence open. It is consequently a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism from \(U'\) onto an open neighborhood of \(X\) in \(V\). It follows (VII 1.5) that \(i\) is a regular immersion.

iii) Suppose \(j\) regular, and that \(V'\) is a vector bundle over \(V\). Since the property to be proved (\(i\) regular) is local on \(V\), we may assume \(V\) affine, \(i\) closed, and that \(V'=V[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\). Set \(V=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
where the morphisms \(u\) and \(v\) are surjective. Let \(t_i\), for \(i=1,\ldots,n\), be elements of \(A\) such that for every \(i\), we have

We define a homomorphism \(w:A[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\longrightarrow A\) by setting \(w(T_i)=t_i\). We thus obtain a closed immersion \(s:V\longrightarrow V'\) which is a section of \(p\), and which is such that \(j=s\circ i\). Moreover, being a section of a smooth morphism, it is a regular immersion (VII 1.10). Knowing that \(s\) and \(s\circ i\) are regular, we will deduce that \(i\) is regular if we show that the sequence:

is exact and locally split, \(I\), \(J\) and \(K\) denoting respectively the ideals defining \(i\), \(j\), and \(s\) (VII 1.7). For this it suffices to show that \(i^*(K/K^2)\) is a direct factor of \(J/J^2\). Now we see at once that \(j'\) is deduced from \(s\) by the base change \(i':X'\longrightarrow V'\). Moreover, the immersions \(j'\) and \(s\) are regular (VII 1.10). Consequently (VII 1.2), the ideal which defines \(j'\) is \(i'^*(K)\), and the conormal sheaf of \(j'\) is \(i^*(K/K^2)\). The exact sequence of conormal sheaves relative to the decomposition \(j=i'\circ j'\) thus gives in particular a homomorphism: \(J/J^2\longrightarrow i^*(K/K^2)\). It is immediate to verify that the composite: \(i^*(K/K^2)\longrightarrow J/J^2\longrightarrow i^*(K/K^2)\) is the identity (from the affine point of view, this corresponds to the decomposition of \(J\) into the direct sum of an ideal of \(A\) and the ideal generated by the \(T_i-t_i\)). We thus indeed have the desired splitting.

iv) Finally, suppose \(j\) regular and \(p\) smooth. For every \(x\in X\), we can find an open neighborhood \(W\) of \(i(x)\), an open neighborhood \(W'\) of \(j(x)\) above \(W\), and a commutative diagram:

where \(p_0\) is the structural morphism, and \(q\) an étale morphism (EGA IV 17.11.4). We may then assume \(V=W\), \(V'=W'\). It is easy to see that \(q\circ j\) is an immersion (introduce the fibered product \(X[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\)). Applying the results ii) and iii), we deduce that \(q\circ i\) is regular, then that \(i\) is regular.

The property being local on \(Z\), we may, in order to prove it, place ourselves in the neighborhood of a point \(x\) of \(X\). We can then find an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) in \(X\), a smooth \(Y\)-scheme \(V\), and a regular \(Y\)-immersion \(i':U\longrightarrow V\). We then have the following commutative diagram:
We deduce from it a closed immersion \(i'':U\longrightarrow Z\times_YV\), and the commutative diagram:
Since \(i'\) is regular, and \(p\) and \(p'\) smooth, we deduce by 1.3 that \(i''\) and \(i\) are regular.
The following proposition will not be used in the rest of the Seminar:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a flat morphism locally of finite presentation. For \(f\) to be a complete intersection morphism in the sense of 1.1, it is necessary and sufficient that it be a complete intersection morphism in the sense of EGA IV 19.3.6.
Suppose that \(f\) is a complete intersection morphism in the sense of 1.1, and let \(x\in X\). Then there exist an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\), and a factorization:

where \(i\) is a regular immersion, and \(g\) a smooth morphism. Let \(y=f(x)\); then the immersion \(i_y:U_y\longrightarrow V_y\) is regular at \(x\). Indeed, we may assume, after shrinking \(V\) if necessary, that there exist a free \(\mathcal O_V\)-Module of finite type \(E\), and a regular homomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_V\) such that the Koszul complex associated to \(u\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal O_U\). We then consider the homomorphism \(u_y\) induced on the fiber at \(y\), and the associated Koszul complex \(K_\bullet(u_y)=K_\bullet(u)_y\). Since \(g\) is smooth, hence flat, \(K_\bullet(u)\) is a \(Y\)-flat resolution of \(\mathcal O_U\). Consequently, the homology groups of \(K_\bullet(u)_y\) are the \(\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathcal O_Y}_i(\mathcal O_U,k(y))\), where \(k(y)\) is the residue field of \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\). Since \(f\) is a flat morphism, they are zero, and \(i_y\) is regular at \(x\). Moreover, since \(g\) is smooth, the local ring of \(V_y\) at \(x\) is regular. Since the local ring of \(U_y\) at \(y\) is a quotient of a regular ring by a regular ideal,
it is a complete intersection ring (EGA IV 19.3.2), and consequently \(f\) is a complete intersection at \(x\) in the sense of EGA IV 19.3.6.

Conversely, suppose that \(f\) is a complete intersection morphism in the sense of EGA IV 19.3.6, and let \(x\in X\). Let \(U\) be an affine neighborhood of \(x\), above an affine neighborhood \(U'\) of \(y=f(x)\). We can then find a smooth \(U'\)-scheme \(V\), and a closed immersion \(U\xrightarrow{i}V\) (by taking for example for \(V\) a vector bundle over \(U'\)). We are going to show that \(i\) is regular. Now by EGA IV 19.3.7, \(i\) is transversally regular at \(x\), since \(V\) is smooth over \(Y\), hence \(V_y\) regular at \(x\), and \(U'\) flat over \(Y\). It then follows from EGA IV 19.2.4, a) \(\Rightarrow\) c), that \(i\) is regular at \(x\). Consequently, \(f\) is a complete intersection morphism in the sense of 1.1.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\), and \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) be two morphisms which are locally complete intersections. Then \(g\circ f\) is a morphism which is locally a complete intersection.
Let \(x\in X\). We can find open neighborhoods \(U\), \(V\) of \(x\), \(f(x)\), and regular immersions \(i\), \(j\) such that we have the factorizations:
We can complete this diagram with the commutative square:

where \(j'\) is obtained from \(j\) by base change; this base change being flat, \(j'\) is a regular immersion, and consequently, \(j'\circ i\) is a regular immersion of \(U\) into a scheme smooth over \(W\), whence the result.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism, \(g:Y'\longrightarrow Y\) a flat base change. If \(f\) is a complete intersection, then the morphism \(f':X\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\longrightarrow Y'\) deduced from \(f\) is one as well. The converse is true if \(g\) is moreover quasi-compact and surjective.

We can find a covering of \(X\) by open sets \(U_i\) such that for every \(i\), the restriction of \(f\) to \(U_i\) factors as \(U_i\longrightarrow V_i\longrightarrow Y\), where \(U_i\longrightarrow V_i\) is a regular immersion, and \(V_i\longrightarrow Y\) a smooth morphism. The \(U_i\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\) form an open covering of \(X\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\), the immersions \(U_i\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\longrightarrow V_i\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\) are regular by VII 1.5, and the morphisms \(V_i\mathbin{\times_Y}Y'\longrightarrow Y'\) are smooth, whence the first result. For the converse, we first conclude from EGA IV 2.7.1 (iv) that \(f\) is locally of finite presentation, which easily reduces us, thanks to 1.2, to the case where \(f\) is a closed immersion, in which case the assertion is none other than VII 1.5.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a complete intersection morphism. Then \(f\) is a perfect morphism.
The property being local on \(X\), we may place ourselves in the neighborhood of a point \(x\) of \(X\), and assume that there exist an open set \(U\) containing \(x\), and a factorization:

where \(i\) is a regular immersion, and \(g\) a smooth morphism. Since \(g\) is smooth, it is a perfect morphism (III 4.1). By VII 1.9, \(i\) is also a perfect morphism. Since the composite of two perfect morphisms is perfect (III 4.5), we deduce the proposition.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a complete intersection morphism, \(x\) a point of \(X\), and consider a factorization:

\[\begin{array}{ccc}U&\xrightarrow{i}&V\\&{}_{f}\searrow&\swarrow{\scriptstyle g}\\&&Y\end{array}\]

where \(U\) is an open neighborhood of \(x\), \(i\) a regular immersion, and \(g\) a smooth morphism. We denote by \(J\) the ideal of \(\mathcal O_V\) defining \(U\). Then the integer

\[d_x=\operatorname{rang}((\Omega^1_{V/Y})_{i(x)})-\operatorname{rang}((J/J^2)_x)\]

is independent of the chosen factorization of \(f\).

Suppose that the restriction of \(f\) to \(U\) factors through two smooth \(Y\)-schemes \(V\) and \(V'\), and set \(V''=V\mathbin{\times_Y}V'\). Then \(V''\) is smooth over \(Y\), and also over \(V\) and \(V'\). Moreover, the immersions \(X\longrightarrow V\) and \(X\longrightarrow V'\) define an immersion \(X\longrightarrow V''\), which is regular by 1.2. We can therefore reduce to comparing the integers \(d_x\) relative to two factorizations of \(f\) through smooth \(Y\)-schemes \(V\) and \(V'\) such that there exists a commutative diagram over \(Y\):
where \(p\) is a smooth morphism. We may moreover assume \(\Omega^1_{V/Y}\), \(\Omega^1_{V'/Y}\), \(\Omega^1_{V'/V}\) of constant rank, as well as the conormal sheaves of \(i\) and \(j\).
From the commutative diagram (where all the morphisms are smooth):

Let \(X'=V'\mathbin{\times_V}X\); then the rank of \(\Omega^1_{V'/V}\) is equal to that of \(\Omega^1_{X'/X}\). Moreover, \(j\) defines a section \(X\xrightarrow{j'}X'\), which is a regular immersion of codimension equal to the rank of \(\Omega^1_{X'/X}\) (EGA IV 17.10.4). If \(i'\) is the immersion deduced from \(i\) by the base change \(V'\longrightarrow V\), we have \(j=i'\circ j'\) and consequently (VII 1.7):

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.9.

The integer \(d_x\) of proposition 1.8 is called the virtual relative dimension of \(X\) over \(Y\) (or of \(f\)) at the point \(x\).

The virtual relative dimension of \(X\) over \(Y\) is thus a locally constant function on \(X\), with integer values (positive or negative). If \(f\) is a smooth morphism of relative dimension \(n\), its virtual relative dimension is \(n\). If \(f\) is a regular immersion of codimension \(n\), its virtual relative dimension is \(-n\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.10.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) and \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) be two complete intersection morphisms, and let \(x\) be a point of \(X\). Then:

\[\operatorname{dim.rel.virt.}_x(g\circ f)=\operatorname{dim.rel.virt.}_x(f)+\operatorname{dim.rel.virt.}_{f(x)}(g).\]
This follows immediately from 1.9 and from the proof of 1.5.

2.1. We shall give here a definition of the cotangent complex which, without having all possible generality (see Exp. 0 4.4), will be sufficient for the needs of the Riemann-Roch theorem; of course, the definition given in exp. 0 coincides in the case considered with the definition given here.
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a smoothable morphism, that is, one admitting a factorization:
where \(g\) is a smooth morphism, and \(i\) an immersion which we may assume closed. Let \(J\) be the ideal of \(X\) in \(V\). The canonical derivation:
which vanishes on \(J^2\), and therefore defines a homomorphism, still denoted \(d\):
One easily verifies that this homomorphism is \(\mathcal O_X\)-linear. We then define a chain complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) by setting:
and by taking as homomorphism \(L^{X/Y}_1\longrightarrow L^{X/Y}_0\) the homomorphism (1.5.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.

The complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) does not depend, up to a canonical isomorphism in the derived category \(D(X)\), on the chosen factorization through a smooth \(Y\)-scheme.

Let \(V\), \(V'\) be two smooth \(Y\)-schemes through which \(f\) factors. Set: \(V''=V\mathbin{\times_Y}V'\); then \(V''\) is smooth over \(Y\), and also over \(V\) and \(V'\). Moreover, the immersions \(X\longrightarrow V\) and \(X\longrightarrow V'\) define an immersion \(X\longrightarrow V''\) which factors \(f\). We can therefore reduce to comparing the complexes \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) relative to two factorizations through smooth \(Y\)-schemes \(V\) and \(V'\), such that there exists a commutative diagram over \(Y\):
where \(i\) and \(j\) are closed immersions, and \(p\) a smooth morphism.
Since \(V\) and \(V'\) are smooth over \(Y\), we obtain the exact sequence:
Since the Modules of differentials considered are locally free on \(\mathcal O_{V'}\), we find by applying the functor \(j^*\) the exact sequence:

Let \(I\), \(J\), \(K\) be the ideals defining \(i\), \(j\), \(j'\); we have: \(j=i'\circ j'\), and the immersions \(i'\), \(j\), \(j'\) are regular; moreover, \(i'\) is defined by the ideal \(p^*(I)\) (since \(p\) is flat), and its conormal sheaf is therefore \(i'^*(p^*(I))=p'^*(I/I^2)\). We then obtain the exact sequence:

a) that the diagram (2.2.1) is commutative; we will thus obtain a morphism of complexes \(\theta\) from the complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) defined by \(V\) to the complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) defined by \(V'\);

b) that the homomorphism: \(K/K^2\longrightarrow j^*(\Omega^1_{V'/V})\) defined by (2.2.2), (2.2.3) and (2.2.1) is an isomorphism; this will imply that the mapping cylinder of \(\theta\) is acyclic, hence that \(\theta\) is a quasi-isomorphism, and therefore defines an isomorphism in the derived category.

The properties a) and b) are of a local nature on \(X\). We may therefore assume \(X\), \(Y\), \(V\) and \(V'\) affine. Set: \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(V=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(V'=\operatorname{Spec}(B')\), \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(C)\), and let us keep the same letters to denote the corresponding homomorphisms and ideals. We have the diagram:

The homomorphism \(I/I^2\longrightarrow J/J^2\) is deduced from the homomorphism: \(IB'\longrightarrow J\) and coincides with the one deduced from \(p:I\longrightarrow J\); the homomorphism: \(i^*(\Omega^1_{V/Y})\longrightarrow j^*(\Omega^1_{V'/Y})\) is deduced from the commutative square (1). Property a) is therefore obvious.

The homomorphism: \(K/K^2\longrightarrow j^*(\Omega^1_{V'/V})\) considered in b) can be defined as follows: let \(\bar x\in K/K^2\), and let \(x\) be an element of \(J\) lifting \(\bar x\) (such an element exists since \(K=J/IB'\)). We associate to \(\bar x\) the image of \(x\) under the composite homomorphism:

Moreover, let \(H\) be the augmentation ideal of: \(B'\otimes_B B'\longrightarrow B'\); the commutative square (2) gives a homomorphism: \(H\longrightarrow K\), whence a homomorphism: \(H/H^2\otimes_{B'}C\longrightarrow K/K^2\). Now \(H/H^2=\Omega^1_{B'/B}\); consequently, it suffices to verify that the homomorphisms constructed are inverse to each other, which is done without difficulty.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a smoothable morphism. The complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\), defined up to canonical isomorphism in the derived category \(D(X)\), is called the relative cotangent complex of \(X\) over \(Y\) (or of \(f\)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a smoothable complete intersection morphism (1.1). Then \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) is perfect. More precisely, for every

factorization of \(f\) as the composite of an immersion and a smooth morphism, the complex \(L^{X/Y}_\bullet\) defined in 2.1 is strictly perfect.

Let \(f=g\circ i\), where \(g\) is a smooth morphism, and \(i\) an immersion. By 1.2, \(i\) is a regular immersion. If \(J\) is the ideal defining \(i\), then \(J/J^2\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type. Likewise, since \(g:V\longrightarrow Y\) is smooth, \(i^*(\Omega^1_{V/Y})\) is locally free of finite type, whence 2.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a smoothable complete intersection morphism, and \(f=g\circ i\) a factorization of \(f\) through a smooth \(Y\)-scheme \(V\). Let \(J\) be the ideal of \(\mathcal O_V\) defining \(i\). Then, if \(K'(X)\) denotes the Grothendieck group of locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type (resp. of perfect complexes on \(X\)), the element \(T_f\in K'(X)\) defined by:

\[T_f=\operatorname{cl}(i^*(\Omega^1_{V/Y}))-\operatorname{cl}(J/J^2)\qquad\text{(resp. }T_f=\operatorname{cl}(L^{X/Y}_\bullet)\text{)}\]

does not depend on the chosen factorization of \(f\). The value at a point \(x\) of \(X\) of the augmentation of \(T_f\) is none other than the virtual relative dimension of \(X\) over \(Y\) at the point \(x\) (1.9).

The element \(T_f\) is well defined in the two cases considered by 2.4, and its class in \(K'(X)\) does not depend on the factorization, by 2.2 in the resp. case, and by the proof of 2.2 in the non-resp. case. The last assertion of 2.5 is trivial.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.6.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\), and \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) be two smoothable complete intersection morphisms, and assume moreover that the following condition is fulfilled:

\(G\) There exists a smooth \(Y\)-scheme \(V\), factoring \(f\), such that for every closed immersion: \(Y\longrightarrow V'\) one can find a smooth \(V'\)-scheme \(V''\), with \(V\simeq V''\mathbin{\times_{V'}}Y\) (this condition is in particular fulfilled if \(f\) is quasi-projective and if \(Y\) has an ample sheaf, by taking for \(V\) a \(\mathbb P_Y^r\)).

Then \(g\circ f\) is a complete intersection, smoothable, and we have a distinguished triangle:

\[\xymatrix{L^{X/Y}_\bullet\ar[dr]&\\L f^*(L^{Y/Z}_\bullet)\ar[rr]&&L^{X/Z}_\bullet.}\]
Let \(V'\) be a smooth \(Z\)-scheme factoring \(g\). We consider the commutative diagram:

Then the immersion \(j'\) is regular, being deduced from \(j\) by a flat base change. Consequently \(j'\circ i\) is a regular immersion factoring \(g\circ f\), which is therefore smoothable and a complete intersection. Moreover, we have the exact sequence of conormal sheaves:

denoting by \(I\), \(J\), \(J'\), \(K\) the sheaves defining respectively the immersions \(i\), \(j\), \(j'\), \(j'\circ i\). Now, \(p''\) being flat, \(J'=p''^*(J)\). The sequence can therefore be written:
Moreover, \(V'\) and \(V''\) being smooth over \(Z\), we have the exact sequence:
Applying \(i^*\circ j'^*\), we find the exact sequence:
Combining (2.6.1) and (2.6.2), we are reduced to seeing that the diagram:
is commutative, which is done locally without difficulty by going back to the definitions of the morphisms involved.
From the exact sequence of complexes thus obtained, we deduce the announced distinguished triangle in the derived category.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7.

With the notations and hypotheses of 2.6, we have in \(K'(X)\) the following equality:

\[T_{g\circ f}=T_f+f^*(T_g).\]
3.1. In section 3, \(Y\) is a quasi-compact scheme having an ample sheaf.
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a projective, complete intersection morphism (1.1); we assume moreover that the virtual relative dimension of \(f\) (1.9)

is constant on \(X\). Since \(f\) is projective, there exists a closed immersion \(X\longrightarrow P\), where \(P\) is a projective bundle over \(Y\), and since \(Y\) has an ample sheaf, we may assume that \(P=\mathbb P_Y^r\). We thus have a factorization:

Since \(f\) is a complete intersection, the immersion \(i\) is regular (1.2). By 1.7, \(f\) is a perfect morphism. Moreover, \(Y\) has an ample sheaf and consequently \(X\) and \(P\) as well. We can then define an additive homomorphism:
(IV 2.12), the \(K'\) defined in terms of locally free sheaves of finite type, or of perfect complexes, being identical. We can also define additive homomorphisms:
Let \(d\) be the virtual relative dimension of \(X\) over \(Y\). The codimension of \(i\) is then \(r-d\). By VII 4.6, the homomorphism \(i_*\) gives, after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\), inclusions:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a projective, complete intersection morphism, whose virtual relative dimension \(d\) is constant. We assume that \(Y\) is quasi-compact, and admits an ample sheaf. We then have for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\):

\[f_*(\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q})\subset\operatorname{Fil}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
We thus obtain in particular an additive homomorphism:

3.3. Recall that we defined in (V 6.1) a functor \(A\longmapsto\operatorname{Ch}(A)\) on the category of graded \(K\)-algebras, with values in the category of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras, \(K\) being a binomial ring (V 2.7). If \(A\) is a graded \(K\)-algebra, we have:
For every augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(\Lambda\), we defined a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism, called the "completed Chern class" (V 6.7):
Finally, we defined a ring homomorphism, functorial in \(A\), called the Chern character (V 6.4):
and a group endomorphism, called the Todd character (V 1.16):

We are going to apply these definitions to the augmented \(\lambda\)-rings \(K'(X)\) and \(K'(Y)\), the augmentation rings \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) and \(H^0(Y,\mathbb Z)\) being binomial (V 2.8). To simplify the notations, we will set, for every scheme \(Z\), and every \(z\in K'(Z)\):

For every graded algebra \(A\) we will regard the group \(1+\widehat A^+\) as contained in \(\widehat A\). Finally, if we have a homomorphism \(f:A\longrightarrow B\), additive, and compatible with the gradings up to a shift, we will again denote by \(f\) the homomorphism \(\widehat A\longrightarrow\widehat B\) deduced from it.

3.4. Let \(X\) be a scheme. For every locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type \(E\), we denote by \(\check E\) the class in \(K'(X)\) of the dual \(\widetilde E\) of \(E\). It is clear that the function which associates \(\check E\) to \(E\) is an additive function on the category of locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type, and consequently defines an endomorphism of \(K'(X)\), which we will denote by \(x\longmapsto\check x\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5.

Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact scheme. Then for every \(x\in K'(X)\) and every \(i\geq0\), we have:

\[c^i(\check x)=(-1)^i c^i(x).\]
The relation to be proved can also be written:

By additivity, we may therefore assume that \(x\) is the class of a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type \(E\). Moreover, up to replacing \(X\) by the flag scheme of \(E\), which gives an injection on the
\(\operatorname{Gr}K'\) (VI 5.5), we may assume that \(E\) is an invertible sheaf. We then have: \(\check E=E^{-1}\); hence \(\check x=x^{-1}\).

The \(c^i(\check x)\), \(c^j(x)\) are zero for \(i,j\geq2\). It therefore suffices to show that \(c^1(x^{-1})=-c^1(x)\). Now \(c^1(x)\) is the class in \(\operatorname{Gr}^1K'(X)\) of \(x-1\), and \(c^1(x^{-1})\) that of \(x^{-1}-1\). The equality:

blocks.label: 3.6. (Riemann-Roch).
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6. (Riemann-Roch).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a projective complete intersection morphism (1.1), whose virtual relative dimension \(d\) is constant (1.9). We assume that \(Y\) is quasi-compact, and admits an ample sheaf. With the notations of 3.3, we then have for every \(x\in K'(X)\) the relation:

\[\operatorname{ch}(f_*(x))=f_{\mathrm{gr}}(\operatorname{ch}(x)\cdot\operatorname{Todd}(\check T_f)),\]

\(\check T_f\) denoting the element dual to the class of the relative cotangent complex \(L_\bullet^{X/Y}\) (2.5 and 2.3).
The proof of theorem 3.6 will occupy the rest of this exposé.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7.

For any \(x\in\operatorname{Gr}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), \(y\in\operatorname{Gr}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\), we have:

\[f_{\mathrm{gr}}(x\cdot f^{\mathrm{gr}}(y))=f_{\mathrm{gr}}(x)\,y.\]

By additivity, we may assume \(x\) and \(y\) homogeneous; let \(x\in\operatorname{Gr}^iK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), \(y\in\operatorname{Gr}^jK'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\). Let \(x'\in\operatorname{Fil}^iK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), \(y'\in\operatorname{Fil}^jK'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\) be elements lifting \(x\) and \(y\). Then the projection formula gives:

By 3.2, this equality holds in \(\operatorname{Fil}^{i+j-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\). We deduce from it an equality in \(\operatorname{Gr}^{i+j-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\), and one immediately verifies that it is indeed the desired equality.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.8.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\), and \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) be two morphisms satisfying the hypotheses of 3.6. Then \(g\circ f\) satisfies them as well. If \(x\) is an element of \(K'(X)\), and if 3.6 holds for \(f\) for the element \(x\), and for \(g\) for the element \(f_*(x)\), then it holds for \(g\circ f\) for the element \(x\).
The functoriality of \(c\) and of Todd implies:
The multiplicativity of the Todd character therefore implies:

3.9. If we apply 3.8 to the factorization of \(f\) given in 3.1, we see that in order to prove the Riemann-Roch theorem, it suffices to prove it in the case of a regular immersion of constant codimension, and in the case of the structural morphism: \(\mathbb P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\).

Furthermore, we may assume that the immersion \(i\) satisfies the condition of VII 4.1. Indeed, let \(s\) be the section of \(\mathbb P_{\mathbb P_Y^r}^2\) introduced in VII 4.2. Then \(s\circ i\) is a regular immersion satisfying the condition of VII 4.1 (VII 4.2) and by 3.8, it suffices to prove 3.6 for \(s\circ i\), \(\mathbb P_{\mathbb P_Y^r}^2\longrightarrow\mathbb P_Y^r\) and \(\mathbb P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\).
We will therefore prove 3.6 by proving the following two particular cases:
i) \(f\) is a regular immersion of constant codimension, satisfying the condition of VII 4.1 ;
ii) \(f\) is the structural morphism \(\mathbb P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\).

pages.title: 4. Riemann-Roch theorem: case of a regular closed immersion

blocks.html: 4. Riemann-Roch theorem: case of a regular closed immersion

4.1. Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion. The Riemann-Roch theorem for \(i\) is essentially the relation VII 4.4, which allows one to compute the Chern classes of \(i_*(x)\) from the \(\gamma^p(N,x)\). More precisely, if we introduce the \(\lambda\)-ring \(K'(Y)_{\mathbb QN}\) defined in V 5.5, then the homomorphism from \(K'(Y)_{\mathbb QN}\) to \(K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) deduced from \(i_*\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism by VII 2.8 and VII 4.3. The Riemann-Roch theorem then reduces to an analogous formula concerning the (additive) homomorphism

defined by \(y\mapsto(0,y)\), that is, to a relation of a formal nature, valid for every \(\lambda\)-ring. We will not develop this proof here, in order to avoid doing yet more formalism on \(\lambda\)-rings (cf. RRR I 1.5), and we will take up again the method of [1] which used blown-up schemes (a method already employed for the proof of VII 4.3, and which is also that of the end of RRR).

As indicated in 3.9, we may restrict ourselves to the case of a regular immersion of constant codimension \(d\) satisfying the condition of VII 4.1, the case of a general regular immersion then being deduced from 3.8. In this section we will take up again the notations of VII 3.1. We thus have the cartesian square:
where \(X'\) is the \(X\)-scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\). We assume moreover that \(X\) is quasi-compact and has an ample sheaf.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.

The morphism \(f\) is projective, a complete intersection, and of virtual relative dimension zero. We have moreover the relation:

\[f_{\mathrm{gr}}\circ f^{\mathrm{gr}}=\operatorname{Id}_{\operatorname{Gr}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}}.\]
The second relation is a consequence of the relation:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.

For every \(y\in K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\), we have the relation:

\[f^{\mathrm{gr}}\circ i_{\mathrm{gr}}(y)=j_{\mathrm{gr}}\bigl(g^{\mathrm{gr}}(y)\cdot c^{d-1}(\check F)\bigr),\]

where \(c^{d-1}\) denotes the \((d-1)\)-th Chern class.
This relation is contained in VII 4.8, for it is none other than \(v^{\mathrm{gr}}\circ u^{\mathrm{gr}}=0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.

Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact scheme. If \(E\) is a locally free sheaf of finite type on \(X\), of class \(E\) in \(K'(X)\), and of rank \(n\), we have:

\[\operatorname{ch}(\lambda_{-1}(E))=c^n(\check E)(\operatorname{Todd}(\check E))^{-1}.\]

The two sides of the relation to be proved are multiplicative: this is clear for \(\operatorname{ch}\circ\lambda_{-1}\), and for Todd; it is also true for \(c^n\), which is the class of \((-1)^{\varepsilon(E)}\lambda_{-1}(E)\). Consequently, we may, by passing to the flag bundle of \(E\), assume that \(E\) is a sum of classes of invertible sheaves, and reduce to showing the formula for an invertible sheaf. Let
\(\xi\) be the class of such a sheaf. We then have:
Since \(\widetilde c(\xi)=(1,1+c^1(\xi))\), we obtain, by the definition of \(\operatorname{ch}\):

Moreover, \(\xi\) has augmentation 1; the Chern class appearing in formula 4.4 relative to \(\xi\) is therefore \(c^1\). Taking into account the definition of Todd, and 3.5, the relation to be proved can be written:

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.4.1.

It is also possible to deduce 4.4 from a corresponding purely algebraic statement, cf. RRR I 1.3.

4.5. We are now going to show that in order to prove the Riemann-Roch theorem in the case considered in this section, one can reduce to the case where \(Y\) is a divisor of \(X\), and where the element to which the formula applies is the inverse image of an element of \(K'(X)\).
Taking into account the functoriality of \(\operatorname{ch}\), and 4.3, it can also be written:
Taking 4.4 into account, the relation to be proved can therefore be written:
Setting \(z=g^*(x)\cdot\lambda_{-1}(F)\), this relation is none other than the Riemann-Roch theorem applied to the regular immersion of codimension 1 \(j\), and to the element \(z\) of \(K'(Y')\).

Moreover, since we assumed that \(i\) satisfies the hypothesis of (VII 4.1), \(\lambda_{-1}(F)\) is divisible by \((1-L)\). We can therefore find \(z'\) such that \(z=z'(1-L)\) is again \(z=j^*(j_*(z'))\) (exp. VII 2.7). We are thus reduced to the case where \(Y\) is a divisor, and to an element \(x\) such that there exists \(y\), with \(x=i^*(y)\).
4.6. We must therefore show the relation:
and from the exact sequence: \(0\longrightarrow J\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow0\), where \(J\) is here invertible, we draw:
Since \(J\) is invertible, we have \(\widetilde c(J)=(1,1+c^1(J))\), hence \(\operatorname{ch}(J)=\exp(c^1(J))\).
Moreover, the functoriality of \(\operatorname{ch}\), and the projection formula 3.7, imply:
Since \(i_*(1)=1-J\), \(i_{\mathrm{gr}}(1)\) is the image in \(\operatorname{Gr}^1K'(X)\) of \(1-J\), so it is \(-c^1(J)\). Moreover, we have:
which proves the relation to be proved.

pages.title: 5. Riemann-Roch theorem: case of the structural morphism of a projective bundle

blocks.html: 5. Riemann-Roch theorem: case of the structural morphism of a projective bundle

5.1. By 3.9, it remains, in order to prove the Riemann-Roch theorem, to prove it for the structural morphism \(f:\mathbb P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\), where \(Y\) is a quasi-compact scheme admitting an ample sheaf. We set \(P=\mathbb P_Y^r\), \(\xi=\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal O_P(1))\), and \(E=(\mathcal O_Y)^{r+1}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.

We have on \(P\) the exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{P/Y}\longrightarrow f^*(E)(-1)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P\longrightarrow0,\]

i.e. we have an isomorphism

\[\Omega^1_{P/Y}\xrightarrow{\sim}F(-1),\qquad\text{où}\qquad F=\operatorname{Ker}(f^*(E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(1)).\]
We consider the vector bundle over \(Y\) defined by \(E\), and we denote by \(Z\) the complement of the zero section of this bundle. We have the commutative diagram:
The morphism \(h\) identifies \(Z\) with the affine \(P\)-scheme defined by the sheaf of algebras \(B=\bigoplus_{n\in\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_P(n)\); \(B\) is a graded \(\mathcal O_P\)-Algebra.

Since \(f\), \(g\), \(h\) are smooth morphisms, we can write the exact sequence of sheaves of relative differentials; since \(Z\) is affine over \(P\), we can write this sequence in terms of \(B\)-Modules; we thus obtain:
Now this exact sequence is a graded exact sequence of graded \(B\)-Modules. Indeed, it results from the commutative diagram:

where the arrows are algebra homomorphisms, compatible with the grading of \(S(E)[T,T^{-1}]\) (the grading defined by the degree with respect to the indeterminate \(T\)), the two other algebras being assumed reduced
The module of differentials \(\Omega^1_{B/\mathcal O_P}\) is isomorphic to \(B(-1)\otimes_{\mathcal O_P}\mathcal O_P(1)\), for we can define a \(S(E)\)-derivation \(d\):

so as to satisfy the universal property which defines \(\Omega^1_{B/\mathcal O_P}\) by setting \(d(aT^n)=naT^{n-1}\otimes dT\); moreover, \(d\) is compatible with the gradings if we assign degree 1 to the factor \(S(E)\,dT\).

Let \(V\) be the vector \(Y\)-bundle defined by \(E\). Then \(\Omega^1_{Z/Y}\) is the restriction to \(Z\) of \(\Omega^1_{V/Y}\). Now \(V\) is the affine \(Y\)-scheme defined by \(S(E)\), so \(\Omega^1_{V/Y}\) is defined by \(S(E)\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}E\) (since \(E\) is locally free of finite type), so \(\Omega^1_{V/Y}\) is equal to \(g^*(E)\), and consequently, \(\Omega^1_{B/Y}=f^*(E)\otimes_{\mathcal O_P}B\). To see its grading, we look locally, and we see at once that \(f^*(E)\) must be regarded as being of degree 1.
Taking the terms of degree 0 in the exact sequence (5.2.1), we find the announced exact sequence.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.2.1.

Using the general results on the differential study of Hilbert functors ([2], 5), one directly obtains an isomorphism \(\Omega^1_{P/Y}\xrightarrow{\sim}F(-1)\) from the functorial characterization given of \(g^*(\Omega^1_{P/Y})\) for every \(Y\)-morphism \(g:Y'\longrightarrow P\).
Since \(E=(\mathcal O_Y)^{r+1}\), we have \(\varepsilon E=r+1\), and consequently:
By the definition of Todd, we thus have:

5.4. Using the basis of \(K'(P)\) over \(K'(Y)\) formed by the elements \(\xi^k\), with \(-r\leq k\leq0\), we see by additivity that it suffices to prove the theorem for elements of the form \(f^*(a)\cdot\xi^k\). We then have:
We thus see that it suffices to prove the theorem for the \(\xi^k\), with \(-r\leq k\leq0\). Now by \(VI (5.2.3)\), we have
Let us now compute the right-hand side of the Riemann-Roch formula.
The right-hand side of the Riemann-Roch formula is therefore:

Since \(x\) is the class in \(\operatorname{Gr}^1K'(P)\) of \(1-\xi^{-1}\), \(f_{\mathrm{gr}}(x^n)\) is, for \(0\leq n\leq r\), the class in \(\operatorname{Gr}^{n-r}K'(Y)\) of \(f_*((1-\xi^{-1})^n)=1\). We thus have:
Recall finally that the elements \(1,x,\ldots,x^{r+1}\) are linearly dependent over \(\operatorname{Gr}K'(Y)\) (VI 5.6), and satisfy the equation:

We can then write the series \(\varphi_k=\exp(kx)\cdot\dfrac{x^{r+1}}{(1-\exp(-x))^{r+1}}\) in a unique way as a linear combination with coefficients in \(\operatorname{Gr}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\) of the elements \(1,\ldots,x^r\). The image under \(f_{\mathrm{gr}}\) of \(\varphi_k\) is then, by (5.4.4) and 3.7, its coefficient on \(x^r\). Taking (5.4.1) into account, the Riemann-Roch theorem for \(f\) is therefore equivalent to:

\[(\mathrm{RR})\qquad\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \text{pour }-r\leq k<0,&\text{le coefficient de }x^r\text{ dans }\varphi_k\text{ est }0\ ;\\ \text{pour }k=0,&\text{le coefficient de }x^r\text{ dans }\varphi_k\text{ est }1. \end{array}\right.\]
This coefficient is the residue of the differential form
Setting \(y=1-\exp(-x)\), we are reduced to computing the residue of:
that is, the coefficient of \(y^r\) in \((1-y)^{-k-1}\). For \(-r\leq k<0\), it is zero, and for \(k=0\), it is 1.
Thus ends the proof of the Riemann-Roch theorem.

[1] A. Borel and J.P. Serre, Le théorème de Riemann-Roch, Bull. Soc. Math. de France, t.86, 1958.

[2] A. Grothendieck, Techniques de construction et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, Sém. Bourbaki, 1960-61.
